title: Quick check: connecting Google Workspace for ITDR

questions.prompt: Which admin role do you need on the customer's Google Workspace side to grant the ITDR integration's OAuth scopes?

choices.label: Global Admin
choices.rationale: That's the M365 term. Using M365 vocabulary on a Workspace call costs you credibility.

choices.label: User-management admin
choices.rationale: A delegated admin. They'll get partway through the flow and Google will reject the scope grant.

choices.label: Domain admin
choices.rationale: Not the named role on the Google side.

questions.explanation: Super-admin is the named Workspace role with the privileges to grant the consent scopes the integration requests.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a real divergence between the M365 and Workspace ITDR integration paths?
choices: Workspace uses Google's OAuth consent screen and may require a domain-wide-delegation step in the Admin console; M365 uses Azure AD enterprise-app consent and doesn't.

choices.label: Workspace ingests events instantly; M365 has a baseline period.
choices.rationale: Inverts what the lesson says. Workspace baseline can actually be longer than M365 for customers with large mailbox histories.

choices.label: Workspace ITDR is billed per identity; M365 ITDR is per tenant.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing and not a real divergence.

choices.label: Workspace ITDR requires a separate Huntress portal; M365 lives in the main portal.
choices.rationale: Both surfaces appear in the same portal under Identity.

questions.explanation: The detection model is unified on the back end; the integration paths diverge at the customer's end.

questions.prompt: "Assuming the Workspace flow will mirror M365" is named as a common mistake. What's the specific risk?
choices: You run the M365 runbook with names swapped, miss the domain-wide-delegation step, and end up with a 'Connected' integration that's silently missing parts of the telemetry.

choices.label: Google will refuse to authorise any integration based on M365 patterns.
choices.rationale: Google doesn't know what the M365 flow looked like; the failure isn't a deliberate Google rejection.

choices.label: Huntress charges a re-onboarding fee if you use the wrong runbook.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial penalty.

choices.label: The OAuth grant locks the customer out of their own admin console.
choices.rationale: OAuth consent grants scopes to the app; it doesn't lock out the granting admin.

questions.explanation: Same partial-state problem as M365 partial consent: looks fine, isn't.

questions.prompt: The customer's IT manager joins the Workspace connection call: "I'm an admin on the Google side, should be quick, right?" What do you do before clicking anything?

choices.label: Run the wizard. If their admin role isn't enough, the wizard will tell you.
choices.rationale: You'll start the flow, the super-admin role check fails partway, and you risk a half-configured integration if some scopes were granted before the failure.

choices: Confirm they're specifically a super-admin, not a delegated or user-management admin, before starting the wizard.

choices.label: Tell them you need a "Global Admin", same as the M365 flow.
choices.rationale: Wrong vocabulary. 'Global Admin' is the M365 term; Workspace uses 'super-admin'.

questions.explanation: The role check costs 30 seconds and prevents the second-failure path.

questions.prompt: The super-admin grants the OAuth scopes. The wizard says "Connected." The runbook also calls for a domain-wide-delegation step in the Google Admin console. The super-admin says: "Cool, are we done? I have a meeting in two minutes." What do you do?

choices.label: Yes, you're done. The wizard says Connected.
choices.rationale: Half the integration is in place. Without domain-wide delegation, parts of the telemetry the SOC expects won't flow.

choices: "Two more minutes, there's one step in the Google Admin console to enable domain-wide delegation, then we're done. Otherwise the telemetry won't be complete."

choices.label: "Don't worry about it, I'll book another call to finish off."
choices.rationale: Booked-but-not-done is where half-installed integrations live for weeks.

questions.explanation: You're respecting the super-admin's time and being explicit about why the extra step matters. Most super-admins will give you the two minutes.

questions.prompt: Which is the right verify check after the Workspace ITDR wizard reports Connected?
choices: Confirm Identity → Google Workspace reads Connected with green status, and that the last-sync timestamp updates within an hour.

choices.label: Confirm the customer has more than one super-admin so you have a backup contact.
choices.rationale: Reasonable customer hygiene, but not the lesson's verify step.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket to confirm the connection registered server-side.
choices.rationale: The portal shows the state directly.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours, then check; the integration won't show anything useful until then.
choices.rationale: Last-sync should update within about an hour; 24 hours is when you'd worry that something stalled.

questions.explanation: Green status is necessary but the moving last-sync is what tells you the pipeline activated, not just the registration.

questions.prompt: The customer's Workspace has third-party-app restrictions that block the consent grant. What do you do?

choices.label: Talk the super-admin into changing tenant policy on the spot so the consent goes through.
choices.rationale: Tenant-wide policy is exactly the wrong thing to change in the moment to unblock yourself.

choices: Bump. Tenant policy changes are a change-management item the senior may already be working.

choices.label: Retry the consent step three or four more times in case it works the next time.
choices.rationale: The block is a policy outcome, not a transient failure.

choices.label: Document the block and skip Workspace coverage.
choices.rationale: Skipping ITDR coverage is a product-mix decision and is itself an escalation trigger.

questions.explanation: Tenant policy is something the senior may already be working with the customer. Bump rather than renegotiate on the call.

questions.prompt: A customer with a large historic mailbox volume asks "how long until we see real identity incidents?" What do you tell them?
choices: Set the expectation that baseline can take longer on Workspace than M365, typically 24 to 72 hours before identity incidents start landing in earnest.

choices.label: Tell them incidents land within minutes of the green status appearing.
choices.rationale: Confuses ingested events with identity incidents. Events flow inside an hour; incidents take the baseline window.

choices.label: Tell them the baseline is the same as M365 (about 24 hours).
choices.rationale: Workspace baseline is typically longer than M365, especially with large mailbox histories.

choices.label: Tell them you'll know when the SOC opens the first incident.
choices.rationale: Sets no expectation, which is the opposite of what the customer needs.

questions.explanation: Set the window so the customer can stand down for that long; they ring you on day one without the expectation set.

questions.prompt: Your customer runs both M365 and Workspace. You just finished the Workspace connection. Why does the verify step include checking M365 status as well?
choices: Mixed tenants are common and missing one of them is a recurring oversight; confirming both are green closes the loop.

choices.label: The two integrations cross-validate each other; both must be green at the same time or neither works.
choices.rationale: The integrations are independent. The verify is about not forgetting the other tenant.

choices.label: Huntress requires both to be reconnected each time one is touched.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: You're charged twice if both aren't green, so it's a billing check.
choices.rationale: Not a billing mechanic.

questions.explanation: Operational hygiene, not commercial. Mixed-tenant customers are a common oversight.

questions.prompt: A delegated admin or user-management admin on Google Workspace can grant the OAuth scopes the ITDR integration requires.
questions.explanation: Only a super-admin has the privileges to grant the consent scopes. A delegated role will get partway through the flow and Google will reject the scope grant.
